trajectory_summary: The call is a low-warmth functional booking. Pike moved through the structural beats in order, collected the bill data, and landed the appointment without a significant derailment. However, the call never built energy — Mark remained passive throughout, the bill data was captured but never emotionally flipped, the trust signal at 05:30 was ignored, and the button-up was incomplete. The appointment is booked but fragile: no verbal handshake, no computer/no-driving anchor, and an unaddressed spam-warning moment that could erode Mark's confidence before the closer calls.
outcome_dispute: The v1 tag is unknown. Based on the transcript, the outcome is a booked appointment — but it should be flagged as conditionally fragile. The button-up is missing three required elements (one-hour block, computer/no-driving anchor, is-that-fair handshake), and the 05:30 trust signal was not resolved. This is a sit that may not hold.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Button-up completion — missing no-driving/computer anchor and is-that-fair handshake
primary_coaching_focus.quote: We'll shoot you a reminder text a little bit before just to double check that time still works.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Pike got the booking but left out the two button-up elements the schema identifies as most predictive of show rate: the computer/no-driving anchor and the is-that-fair verbal handshake. With brothers on the title unconfirmed for the appointment and a possible unresolved trust signal at 05:30, this sit is already fragile — the missing button-up elements remove the last structural protection against a no-show. This is the highest-leverage fix because it directly protects Pike's paycheck on this specific sit.

key_moments.quote: What got you interested in looking at solar?
key_moments.stewart_read: Pike asked the right question but accepted a one-line answer ('We would like to install the solar') without any depth probe. The schema is explicit: this is the emotional anchor, and top setters spend 60+ seconds here. Pike moved on in under 10 seconds. The bill was later captured as data ($80/month, 505 kWh) but was never flipped into emotional alignment against the utility. Reactive read holds: this was a checkbox moment, not an anchor moment.

key_moments.quote: Just just in in the balance and me and my brothers.
key_moments.stewart_read: Mark discloses that brothers are co-owners on the title. This is a co-decision-maker signal that requires the same protocol response as a spouse disclosure: include the co-owners in the appointment. Pike acknowledged it ('K. Perfect.') and moved on without asking whether the brothers needed to be present. The appointment was booked with only Mark confirmed. Reactive read holds: Pike treated this as a data point rather than a protocol trigger.

key_moments.quote: It should say this is lying but not real.
key_moments.stewart_read: This line is likely Mark reading a spam or fraud warning that appeared on the incoming Ion text. If genuine, it is a scam_anxiety_signal — Mark's phone flagged the text as suspicious, and Mark read it aloud. Pike's response was silence followed by a confirmation question about whether the text came through. The trust signal was not addressed at all. Note: transcript quality is uncertain here — this line may be a Deepgram artifact. Flag for audio review before coaching on it. If the utterance is genuine, the schema's scam_anxiety_signal directive applies: the rep should have acknowledged the warning and reframed the appointment as customer-initiated.

key_moments.quote: Let me just try shooting you in from my personal number then because I don't know why it wasn't not popping up.
key_moments.stewart_read: When the system text failed, Pike's only fallback was his personal phone number. This is the anti-pattern the schema explicitly flags: personal number exposure creates privacy and scale risk for the floor. Notably, Pike had already successfully extracted a verbal readback of monthly kWh figures (04:07–04:47) before pivoting to the photo request — meaning he abandoned a working path (verbal readback) and then fell back to personal number exposure when the photo path failed. The reactive read holds on both counts: the pivot to photo was unnecessary given the verbal data already in hand, and the personal number fallback is a known anti-pattern.
